text:
Olympics



Track and field gold medalists in this summer’s Paris Olympics will receive $50,000 in prize money from the sport’s governing body, marking the first time an international federation has paid athletes for Olympic success.

World Athletics, the international governing body overseeing track and field, said Wednesday a $2.4 million pot will be distributed across the 48 track and field events, with the money coming from the International Olympic Committee’s revenue share allocation. Individual winners will receive the full $50,000 while top relay teams will split the prize money among members.

Advertisement

World Athletics plans to expand the prize pool to include Olympic silver and bronze medalists at the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles, it said in a news release.



World Athletics President Sebastian Coe said the prize money is about “empowering the athletes and recognizing the critical role they play.”

“While it is impossible to put a marketable value on winning an Olympic medal, or on the commitment and focus it takes to even represent your country at an Olympic Games, I think it is important we start somewhere and make sure some of the revenues generated by our athletes at the Olympic Games are directly returned to those who make the Games the global spectacle that it is,” Coe said in a statement.

Many countries, including the United States, pay Olympic medalists. At the Tokyo Games, the United States Olympic and Paralympic Committee gave gold medalists $37,500, silver medalists $22,500 and bronze medalists $15,000. Seven of Team USA’s 39 golds in 2020 came from track and field events.

But the IOC and each sport’s governing bodies have not traditionally paid out winners, making Wednesday’s announcement a fundamental change for track and field moving forward.

It’s unclear whether track and field Olympians who plan to begin or continue competing at the collegiate levelwill be able to accept the World Athletics prize money while maintaining eligibility. TheNCAAdid not immediately return a request for comment. Though college athletes can receive payments by partnering with brands to advertise products or appear at promotional events under the NCAA’s name, image and likeness (NIL) rules, getting direct pay for athletic achievements would violate NCAA eligibility rules.

NCAA bylaws allow athletes to receive prize money from the USOPC while keeping their collegiate eligibility intact, but accepting the same type of payment from individual sports organizations is currently off-limits. Thatdistinction is the basis of a lawsuit that Reese Brantmeier, a student at the University of North Carolina who is ranked No. 2 in singles and No. 1 in doubles (with partner Elizabeth Scotty) by the Intercollegiate Tennis Association, filed against the NCAA in U.S. District Court in North Carolina. Brantmeier is asking the judiciary to prohibit the NCAA from prohibiting individual sport athletes like her from collecting prize money.

The Paris Olympics begin July 26 and run through Aug. 11. Athletics events start on Aug. 1.

(Photo: Rob Carr / Getty Images)

title: Olympic track and field gold medalists to receive prize money from World Athletics for first time - The Athletic
author: Tess DeMeyer
description: World Athletics will award $50,000 in prize money to first-place finishers with plans to pay silver and bronze medalists in 2028 Games.
keywords: College Sports, Olympics, Top Sports News
